INF10024 Business Digitalisation



Mark Dale and Rohan Bennett
February 2023

                                          Week 1 : Making a Start
                                   1. Introduction and Overview



                         Week 2 to 4 : What and Who are Information Systems?
      2. Digital Age                   3. Think Like a Process                    4. The Tech Sector                          Assignment 1 and 2

5. Beyond ERP              6. IT Infrastructure          7. Data & Analytics             8. SDLC & Agile              Assignment 3



                        Week 9 to 11 : Where to next with Information Systems?
9. Liquid Enterprises                      10. Cybersecurity                    11. Global and Green                                      Assignment 4





                                           Week 12 : Close Out
                                              12. Wrap-Up

                Business                         Information                              Systems
                 Issues                             Issues                                 Issues

What ’ s  on  t oday’ s  menu?


Understand the concept       of the Digital Age


Appreciate the role of technology       innovation in business transformation


Explain the interconnected nature of organisations


Explain competitive advantage and why it is critical to transformation


Describe and apply PEST, SWOT        analysis and Porter  ’s Five Forces Model

The Digital Age
When? What? Why?

What   i s  ‘di gi tali sati on’?


First, let ’s get the terminology     clear


Digit = any single numeral, but, 0 or 1 in the case of computers


Digitisation   = conversation of text, picture or sound into 0s and 1s, processable by computers


Digitalisation   = changing of processes (inc. business processes) drivenby               digital data and digital
technologies


Digital Transformation = the radical redesign, renewal, or development of a business or a sector,
driven by digital technologies (e.g., new products and services)







                                                                                                                                             5

Has the ‘ Di gi tal  Age’  arri ved?


It’s been a long time coming

   Capital    Investment
      • The accumulating global value of digital capital investments has reached more than US$6 trillion in 2013,
        which is equivalent to 8.5 % of nominal world GDP
   Connectivity
      • According to Gartner (2014), by 2020, more than 7 billion people and businesses, and at least 30 billion
        devices will be connected to the Internet
   Critical Mass
      • In 2013 there were 6.8 billion mobile-cellular subscriptions with 7.1 billion global population, 2.7 billion
        global Internet users (around 40 % of world’s population), and 41 % of households (around 750 million
        households) in the world connected to the Internet

What   i s  t he  Di gi t al   Age?



    Data
       •  The most valuable asset, and the richest businesses in the world own it
    Delivery
       •  Most service and transactions are fully digital
    Demand
       •  Citizens expect and demand ubiquitous internet connectivity
    Devices
       •  There are billions, all connected to the internet, with dozens per person
    Dualism
       •  We   h a v e   d u a l   ( c o n v e rg i n g )   d i g i t a l   a n d   p h y s i c a l   ex i s t e n c e s   a n d   i d e n t i t i e s

Why  di d  i t   happen?


  Te      c      h      n      o      l      o      g      y       Push…
    • Semi-conductors, microprocessors and Moore's Law (60s)
    • From ARPAnet to Internet (70s to 90s)
    • Personal computers to smart phones (80s to 2000s)
    • From analogue to digital media (90s to 2000s)
  Society Pull…
    • Faster, better, stronger, …now
    • From nerds to the world's richest people (G’day Bill and Mark)
    • …towards sustainability and ‘greenness’


                                              Does ‘why  ’ really matter?

What   does  t he  Di gi t al   Age  mean  for   busi ness?





                         Opportunities                          Threats
                         • Exploit: new ways to do old          • Inertia: failure to adapt and
                           things better (e.g. online             change, but change costs!
                           banking)                             • Replacement: new products
                         • Rethink: Ways of doing                 and services to compete
                           things faster and cheaper              with
                           (e.g. Amazon)                        • Competition: new
                         • Invent: New things                     competitors, a global
                           altogether (e.g. Twitter)              marketplace


                        Is there even a choice? All businesses must assess and reassess how
                                          they will transform and compete…

Transformation and
Innovation

Diffusion and Hype Cycles

How does busi ness transformati on happen?


It’s not just a matter of procuring some server space and a few computers!


Te     c     h    n    o     l     o     g     y           o     n          i     t     s           o   w     n     will not transform a business, especially a large one


We   n e e d   a   mo re  holistic and strategic approach to organisational change


It needs to be sponsored by management and supported                                      by   st af f ,   cust ome r s,   and   ot he r
stakeholders


We   h a v e   w h o l e   u n i t s   d e a l i n g   w i t h   t h i s   l a t e r   i n   o u r  IS courses, but let’s cover some basics…

    What   i s  ‘ t echnol ogy  di ffusi on’   t heor y?


    Adoption      of a new idea, behaviour, or product (i.e., "innovation") does not happen
    simultaneously in a social system; rather it is a process                      whereby some people are more apt to
    adopt the innovation than others.















































See: Rogers, Everett (16 August 2003). Diffusion of Innovations, 5th Edition. Simon and Schuster. ISBN 978-0-7432-5823-4.

Exampl e: Googl e Gl ass




Back in the early 2010s, Google Glass failed           due to unsure purpose
and privacy concerns


The concept is starting to make a comeback in the manufacturing
and warehousing sector (e.g. Trimble)


Boeing adopted Glass for a purpose. They figured that smart
ey ewe ar   coul d   cut   d own  on  t rai ni ng  t i me   and   si mp l i fy   t he i r
complicated assembly processes by removing paper manuals and
freeing peoples’ hands


Early adopters?

What   i s  t he  ‘ Hype  Cycl e’ ?


Developed by Gartner


Used to represent the
maturity, adoption and
social application of specific
technologies


The idea is that every
emerging technology goes
through a number of
perception phases over time




















See: https://www.gartner.com/en/research/methodologies/gartner-hype-cycle

Exampl e: Drone Del i veri es


















Hype or feasible?

Exampl e: Checkout-less Supermarket


The system is logging the items as the shopper
goes along, which eliminates the need to go
through a traditional check-out line


When customers exit the store through a
“transition area,” the system senses that they ’re
leaving, adds up the items and charges their
Amazon account


Hype or feasible?

Exampl e: Augmented Real i ty
Shopping



AR makes it possible to see virtual products in your actual
environment,


The integration of AR in eCommerce is quickly changing how
consumers shop by allowing them to see what they want to
buy before any purchase is made


Hype of feasible?

Competitive
Advantage

Analyse> Strategize > Sustain

What   i s  ‘ Compet i t i ve  Advant age’ ?


Along with understanding how technology is adopted, businesses also need to understand
themselves and their uniqueness          in the surrounding environment


Competitive advantage describes the re l at i v e   p os i t i on of firms in a particular market


Competitive advantage refers to an         integrated set of actions aimed at increasing the long-term
well being and strength of the enterprise relative to competitors (Ward and Peppard, 2002)


The aim is to make competitive advantage sustainable


Note: being first does not always mean being sustainable

How do we i denti fy our competi ti ve advantage?

What   t ool s  do  we  have  avai l abl e  t o  us?



 Numerous and Va r ie d

    •Internal organization
    •External environment
    •Combination of the two perspectives
 Heuristic not Guaranteed

    •They do not provide 100% confidence as predictive tools
 Some have been around for a while (because they are good)

    •SWOT
    •PEST
    •Va lu e  C h a in
    •Five Forces Model

What   i s  a  PEST  anal ysi s?


It unpacks the ex t e r nal   context and forces that a
business operates it

These usually cannot       be controlled                                        Political                         Economic
                                                                                • Government                      •GDP
                                                                                • Laws                            •Export / Import
But, can we influences       them? Sometimes –         absolutely i.e.
PEST becomes PETS

Analysing    each element helps us to understand                                Social                            Te     c     h     n     i     c     a     l
constraints and opportunities                                                   • Cultural norms                  •Infrastructure
                                                                                • Education levels                •Access

Sometimes      Legal and Environmental are added to make
PESTLE

What   i s  SWOT  anal ysi s?


A SWOT (strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats)
analysis looks at internal and external factors that can affect a
business


Can be detailed and informed by market research, industry,
segment supplier, customer research or it can be a ‘back of
envelope’ activity


To           b     e      effective, you need to baseline your analysis against a
strategic driver  e.g    a new university campus, a new course, a
new product, meger        and acquisition, …..

What   i s  ‘Porter’ s Five Forces' framework?


                            Considers the ex t e r n a l   c o mp e t i t i v e   e n v i ro n me n t


                            Focuses on the business domain or industry in which an company operates and
                            identifies the business pressures that may be brought to bear on that company


                            Usually applied to the products and/ or services delivered by a company



                            Premise: companies ex i s t   wi t h i n   a n   i n d u s t r y   a n d   t o   s u c c e e d ,   t h ey   mu s t   e f f e c t i v e l y
                            deal with the competitive forces that exist within their particular industry


                            The competitive forces are ex t e n s i o n s   o f   t h e   i n t e ra c t i o n  between the company and
                            its customers, suppliers, new entrants, substitute products and competitors

                                               New Entrants


         Threat of potential
               entrants


                                                                                Bargaining
                                                                                 power of
                                                                                  buyers
Suppliers                                                                                               Buyers

                                              Intensity of Rivalry
                                                     Among
              Bargaining power                    Companies
                 of suppliers
                                                                        Threat of substitute
                                                                           products and
                                                                               services


                                                 Substitutes

What   quest i ons  must   we  ask?



 Industry competitors
     • What is the level of competition for products and services in the industry ?
     • Is the company in a good competitive position or is it a minor player?
     • Do one or more companies have a dominant position within the market?
 New Entrants
     • Are there barriers to entry such as capital investment, access to channels, learning curve advantages, etc.?
     • Is it possible to set up a company offering the same goods and services without much financial support?
     • What is the likelihood of new entrants coming into the industry anew or through forwards or backwards
       integration?
 Substitutes
     • What is the range of substitute products and services available?
     • What is the competitive position of the company when compared to the firms offering these products and
       services?

What   quest i ons  must   we  ask?



 Buyers

    • How much choice do buyers have?
    • Can they switch suppliers easily?
    • Do they have power in the relationship or are they locked into the supplier (i.e. the concentration
      of suppliers is greater in relation to the concentration of buyers)?
 Suppliers

    • How many suppliers are available?
    • Is this a competitive situation where the company has a choice of suppliers?
    •  Do the suppliers have power in the relationship because they operate in an area of limited supply?

Example for Swinburne University
